title: SAE-GUIDE: Sparse Autoencoder-Guided Uncertainty-aware Information Detection and Enhancement for Multi-Hop Retrieval
description: SAE-GUIDE uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose LLM hidden states during multi-hop reasoning into interpretable sparse features, identifying specific information gaps in real-time to trigger targeted retrieval. Unlike binary active retrieval methods (Probing-RAG) that only decide whether to retrieve, SAE-GUIDE determines both when and what information is missing by detecting feature activation patterns that correspond to specific knowledge needs. Method renamed from SAE-PROBE to avoid conflict with existing SAE-Probes work.
motivation: Current multi-hop retrieval methods either generate explicit reasoning (high latency), use binary probes on hidden states (cannot identify what information is missing), or rely on latent self-distillation (requires full model training). While Xin et al. (ACL 2025) show SAE features can steer RAG behavior through intervention, no method uses SAEs for automated real-time information-need detection to trigger retrieval. SAE-GUIDE fills this gap by using sparse features to identify specific information needs during reasoning, distinguishing between steering (manual control) and detection (automated triggering).
proposed_approach: SAE-GUIDE operates in four phases: (1) Train SAEs on intermediate-layer hidden states from multi-hop reasoning examples to extract sparse, interpretable features. (2) Identify features that correlate with specific information gaps (entity needs, temporal facts, relational knowledge) and learn feature-to-query mappings. (3) Train lightweight probes on cumulative SAE feature activations to detect when information is missing. (4) At inference, when cumulative activation exceeds a threshold, identify top-activated features, map them to query augmentations using learned mappings, and retrieve targeted information. The approach uses a frozen LLM (Qwen2.5-7B), training only the SAE (~100M params) and probe (~30M params). Includes explicit failure analysis for cases where SAE features may not be interpretable or correlate with needs.
key_innovations: First automated detection system using SAE features for real-time information-need detection (vs. steering/intervention in Xin et al. ACL 2025; vs. post-hoc detection in RAGLens)
Interpretable sparse features identify WHAT information is missing, not just WHETHER retrieval is needed
Concrete feature-to-query mapping with examples showing how entity/temporal/relation features translate to retrieval augmentations
Lightweight alternative to full model training (LaSER): frozen LLM with small trainable components
Cumulative feature activation captures information needs across reasoning steps
Systematic failure mode analysis (F1-F5) addressing interpretability, correlation, and generalization risks

related_work.relation: Xin et al. use SAE features to manually steer RAG behavior (context vs. memory prioritization). SAE-GUIDE differs by using SAE features for automated detection of information needs to trigger retrieval. Key distinction: steering (intervention/control) vs. detection (automated triggering).

related_work.paper: LaSER: Internalizing Explicit Reasoning into Latent Space for Dense Retrieval
related_work.relation: LaSER uses self-distillation to align latent tokens with explicit CoT. SAE-GUIDE differs by using frozen LLM with SAE decomposition for interpretability rather than full model training with latent tokens.

related_work.paper: Probing-RAG: Self-Probing to Guide Language Models in Selective Document Retrieval
related_work.relation: Probing-RAG trains binary classifiers on hidden states for retrieval decisions. SAE-GUIDE advances this with SAE decomposition providing interpretable features and finer-grained information-need detection.

related_work.paper: Are Sparse Autoencoders Useful? A Case Study in Sparse Probing
related_work.relation: Engels et al. evaluate SAEs for sparse classification tasks, finding limited benefits over raw hidden states. SAE-GUIDE addresses different problem (information-need detection) and uses cumulative activation patterns rather than simple linear probes. Method name changed from SAE-PROBE to avoid conflict.

related_work.paper: Toward Faithful Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Sparse Autoencoders (RAGLens)
related_work.relation: RAGLens uses SAEs for post-hoc hallucination detection. SAE-GUIDE uses SAEs for real-time information-need detection to trigger proactive retrieval before errors occur.

related_work.paper: Unified Active Retrieval for Retrieval Augmented Generation (UAR)
related_work.relation: UAR uses multiple binary classifiers for different retrieval criteria. SAE-GUIDE uses continuous feature activations for more nuanced, interpretable detection.

related_work.paper: HopRAG: Multi-Hop Reasoning for Logic-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation
related_work.relation: HopRAG uses graph traversal for multi-hop retrieval. SAE-GUIDE focuses on interpreting LLM internal states rather than external graph structure.

hypothesis: Using SAE-decomposed hidden state features for information-need detection will improve multi-hop QA accuracy over binary probe methods (Probing-RAG) by enabling more targeted retrieval, while requiring less computation than full model training approaches (LaSER). Automated detection based on feature activations can effectively trigger retrieval without manual intervention (unlike Xin et al.'s steering approach).

confirmation: Statistically significant improvement over Probing-RAG baseline (p < 0.05)
15%+ reduction in retrieval calls vs. periodic retrieval (IRCOT) with comparable accuracy
>50% of top SAE features rated as interpretable/meaningful
SAE features outperform binary probe baseline in ablation
refutation: No improvement over simple binary probe baseline
Feature interpretability <30% (features don't capture semantic needs)
Cumulative activation provides no benefit over per-step activation
Feature-to-query mapping does not improve retrieval precision

datasets: HotpotQA: 500 questions from dev set (2-hop reasoning)
baselines: Probing-RAG (binary MLP probe on hidden states)
LaSER-inspired: Contriever with query expansion (simplified comparison)
ablations: Early vs. mid vs. late layer features
Feature type ablation (entity vs. temporal vs. all)

timeline.hour_1_2: Setup, data prep, retrieval index construction
timeline.hour_8: Feature interpretability analysis, failure mode analysis, documentation

novelty_claims: First automated detection system using SAE features for real-time information-need detection (distinct from Xin et al. ACL 2025's steering/intervention approach)
First work to map SAE feature activations to targeted retrieval queries with concrete examples
Lightweight alternative to LaSER: frozen LLM + small trainable components vs. full model training
Systematic failure mode analysis for SAE-based retrieval detection
potential_impact: Reduced retrieval costs through targeted information-need detection
Interpretable retrieval decisions for debugging and transparency
Model-agnostic approach applicable to any LLM with extractable hidden states
Guidance for future SAE-based retrieval systems through failure mode analysis
limitations: 8-hour constraint limits dataset size (800 questions vs. full benchmarks)
Cannot fully train LaSER for direct comparison (use simplified proxy)
Automated rather than extensive human evaluation of feature interpretability
Small dataset (800 questions) limits generalizability - acknowledged as significant limitation
Limited hyperparameter search due to compute constraints

failure_modes.F1: SAE features not interpretable - Detection: human evaluation; Mitigation: adjust sparsity
failure_modes.F2: Features don't correlate with information needs - Detection: correlation analysis; Mitigation: try different layers
failure_modes.F3: Cumulative activation provides no benefit - Detection: ablation; Mitigation: simplify to per-step
failure_modes.F4: Feature-to-query mapping fails - Detection: retrieval precision comparison; Mitigation: heuristic mappings
failure_modes.F5: Overfitting to training distribution - Detection: cross-dataset evaluation; Mitigation: more diverse data

method_rename_note: Renamed from SAE-PROBE to SAE-GUIDE to avoid conflict with JoshEngels/SAE-Probes (GitHub). SAE-GUIDE stands for 'Sparse Autoencoder-Guided Uncertainty-aware Information Detection and Enhancement'.
distinction_from_xin_2025: Xin et al. (ACL 2025) demonstrate that SAE features CAN BE USED to steer RAG behavior through manual intervention. SAE-GUIDE asks whether SAE features AUTOMATICALLY SIGNAL information needs to trigger retrieval without human intervention. This is the difference between steering (control/intervention) and detection (automated monitoring/triggering).
publication_target: ACL, EMNLP, or NAACL (top-tier NLP venues)
